credit-card.notes: California courts treat credit card debt as a written contract, subject to the 4-year limitation under CCP § 337. The clock typically starts on the date of the first missed payment. California's Rosenthal Fair Debt Collection Practices Act extends FDCPA-like protections to original creditors as well as collectors.
credit-card.citation: Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 337; Cal. Civ. Code §§ 1788–1788.33 (Rosenthal FDCPA)

medical.notes: Medical debts based on written agreements are subject to California's 4-year limitation. California has enacted SB 1061 (2022) which provides significant medical debt protections, including prohibiting reporting medical debt to credit bureaus.
medical.citation: Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 337; Cal. Health & Safety Code § 127400 et seq.

auto-loan.notes: Auto loan deficiency balances are subject to California's 4-year limitation for written contracts. Note: California prohibits deficiency judgments on purchase money mortgages (one-action rule).

personal-loan.notes: Personal loans in California are subject to the 4-year limitation for written contracts, or 2 years for oral agreements (CCP § 339).

student-loan.notes: Private student loans in California are subject to the 4-year limitation. California has enacted the Student Loan Servicing Act providing additional protections. Federal loans are not subject to state SOL for government collection.
student-loan.citation: Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 337; Cal. Fin. Code § 28100 et seq.

mortgage-deficiency.notes: California's anti-deficiency laws (CCP §§ 580b, 580d) prohibit deficiency judgments on purchase money mortgages and non-judicial foreclosures. For judicial foreclosures where a deficiency is allowed, the action must be brought within 3 months after the foreclosure sale.
mortgage-deficiency.citation: Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 726; Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 580b; Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 726(b)

utility-bill.notes: Utility bills based on a written service agreement are subject to California's 4-year limitation period.

regulatoryBody: California Department of Financial Protection and Innovation (DFPI) & California Attorney General
complaints: File a complaint with the CA DFPI at dfpi.ca.gov or the CA AG at oag.ca.gov. California's Rosenthal FDCPA (Civil Code § 1788) covers both original creditors and debt collectors.
